145.manipulation: Followed the egg's natural pigment 'zenith field' mark and added artificial marks, hair-ligature constrictions and punctures to uncleaved alpine-newt eggs.
145.design: Continuous observation of individually marked eggs through cleavage, with constriction and puncture interventions; results compared against the amphibian literature (Table β).
145.readout: Position of the embryo's median plane relative to the egg's mark and to the first cleavage furrow.
145.finding: The first cleavage furrow does not reliably coincide with the median plane; the embryo's axis is fixed relative to the natural pigment mark rather than the furrow.
145.summary: Przibram exploited a natural pigment spot (the 'zenith field') on the alpine-newt egg, plus artificial marks, hair ligatures and punctures, to track identifiable points on the egg through cleavage. Across 190 eggs he scored how the first furrow related to the later median plane, establishing that the furrow is an unreliable guide and that the embryo's position is set by the egg's intrinsic marks — a marking method Vogt's vital-dye fate-mapping would soon supersede.

38.manipulation: Castrated young rats and guinea pigs and implanted the opposite sex's gonad, or both glands together, to feminize, masculinize, or produce hermaphrodites.
38.design: Treatment animals versus normal controls; experimental sex reversal and induced double-sex ('hermaphrodite') glands.
38.readout: Development of secondary sexual characters, body form, mating behaviour, and gonad histology.
38.n: Series of implanted rodents (rats, guinea pigs)
38.finding: The gonad's interstitial 'puberty gland' secretes the hormones that determine secondary sex characters; implanting the opposite gland reverses or doubles them, yielding experimental hermaphrodites.
38.summary: Steinach removed the gonads of immature rodents and grafted in the opposite gland — or both — then watched the sexual characters, body form and behaviour that developed. The reversals and induced hermaphrodites led him to locate sex-determining hormone secretion in the gonad's interstitial 'puberty gland', founding the experimental endocrinology of sex.

70.manipulation: Amputated newt forelimbs after displacing the skeleton out of place or removing it entirely (skeleton-less stumps), then let them regenerate.
70.design: Comparison of regenerates from normal, out-of-place-skeleton, and skeleton-less stumps.
70.readout: Whether and how a correctly patterned limb skeleton re-forms in the regenerate.
70.n: Series of operated Triton cristatus limbs
70.finding: Limb regeneration is independent of the pre-existing skeleton: the regenerate's skeleton forms anew from the blastema under a regional 'determination field'.
70.summary: Weiss amputated newt limbs whose skeleton he had either displaced or removed, and showed that a normal limb — skeleton included — regenerated regardless. The skeleton is therefore not the template; the regenerate's pattern is imposed by a region-specific 'determination field' acting on the blastema. The result travelled into modern axolotl limb-regeneration biology.

135.manipulation: Reared praying mantises from hatching, weighing and measuring body length at each instar and moult.
135.design: Longitudinal measurement across successive instars; tabulation and fitting of growth curves.
135.readout: Body length and mass at each instar, and the ratios between successive instars.
135.n: Reared Sphodromantis bioculata, measured each instar
135.finding: Growth proceeds in regular stepwise ratios per moult — the empirical basis of what became 'Przibram's Rule' (~2.09x mass, ~1.26x linear dimension per instar).
135.summary: Przibram and Megušar turned growth into numbers: rearing mantises and recording length and weight at every moult. The regular inter-instar ratios they extracted became 'Przibram's Rule', a generalization still tested by name in arthropods today — the clearest instance of the BVA's drive to make biology quantitative.

39.manipulation: Forced the midwife toad to change its reproductive mode by altering temperature, humidity and 'epidemic' conditions, then bred the modified animals.
39.design: Control series versus descendant series across generations.
39.readout: Whether the enforced reproductive behaviour and associated structures reappear in the offspring.
39.finding: Kammerer reported that the environmentally enforced reproductive adaptations were inherited by the descendants — a Lamarckian claim, later disputed (the 1926 nuptial-pad scandal).
39.summary: Kammerer changed the midwife toad's mode of reproduction by manipulating its environment, then bred the altered animals and compared descendants with controls, reporting that the enforced adaptation was inherited. The corpus reports the claim as he stated it; it became the most contested experiment of the era and the centre of the modern epigenetics-revival debate.

56.manipulation: Kept freshwater polyps and examined the green hydra's relationship to its symbiotic algae, together with feeding, digestion and responses.
56.design: Exploratory preliminary experiments and observations on Hydra biology.
56.readout: The algal symbiosis, intracellular digestion, budding and responses of the polyp.
56.n: Preliminary series (incomplete by the author's own note)
56.finding: Documented the green hydra's symbiosis with Zoochlorella and basic features of its feeding and biology as groundwork for an experimental biology of the polyp.
56.summary: Hadži's 'preliminary experiments' surveyed the biology of Hydra — above all the green hydra's partnership with its symbiotic algae, plus digestion and responses. Explicitly unfinished, it nonetheless became the early reference point for the hydra-alga symbiosis that modern work on algal transmission and host-symbiont biology still cites.

44.manipulation: Reared the alpine and fire salamanders under altered conditions (altitude, moisture, temperature) and recorded reproductive mode and body characters.
44.design: Comparative, statistical study across the two species and across conditions, with experimental induction of altered reproduction.
44.readout: Reproductive mode (live larvae vs fully-formed young), larval form, coloration and morphology, and their heritability.
44.n: Large statistical series across both species
44.finding: Kammerer argued the two salamanders' reproductive and morphological differences are environmentally induced, heritable adaptations (a Lamarckian / atavism claim, later disputed).
44.summary: In this large statistical monograph Kammerer reared the two Salamandra species under altered conditions and recorded how their reproduction, larval form and colour responded, arguing that the differences between the species are environmentally induced and heritable. It is an early, data-heavy statement of the neo-Lamarckian program that defined — and ultimately undid — his reputation.

93.manipulation: Kept normal and blinded crustaceans on backgrounds of different colours, in daylight and in darkness, and observed their chromatophores.
93.design: Series crossing background colour x light condition x intact/blinded animals; macroscopic and microscopic observation across the day-night cycle.
93.readout: Expansion and contraction of the chromatophores and the resulting body colour.
93.n: Four crustacean genera (Gelasimus, Potamobius, Palaemonetes, Palaemon)
93.finding: Colour change follows background and light and depends on the eyes — blinded animals fail to adapt — implicating a visually mediated chromatophore response.
93.summary: Megušar crossed background colour, illumination and blinding to dissect colour change in four crustaceans, observing the chromatophores directly. Showing that intact eyes are required for background adaptation, the work is an early analysis of the visually driven, hormonally mediated chromatophore physiology that crustacean colour-change research still pursues.

148.manipulation: Grafted supernumerary developed limbs onto amphibians and observed their innervation and movement.
148.design: Observation of the grafted limb's motor behaviour relative to the host's own homologous limb.
148.readout: Whether, and in what timing, the transplanted limb moves relative to the host's limbs.
148.finding: Transplanted limbs move in exact synchrony with the host's homologous limb regardless of which nerves reach them — Weiss's 'resonance' principle of motor coordination (myotypic specificity).
148.summary: Weiss grafted extra limbs onto amphibians and found that each moved in perfect unison with the host's corresponding limb, whatever nerve supplied it. He read this 'homologous response' as evidence that muscles impose their identity on their nerves — the 'resonance' / myotypic-specificity principle that became a cornerstone of developmental neuroscience and the neural-specificity debate.

9.manipulation: Amputated newt limbs through only part of the cross-section (e.g., reaching only to the mid-lower-leg), leaving a partial wound surface.
9.design: Comparison of regenerate completeness from partial versus full limb cross-sections.
9.readout: Whether a whole, correctly patterned limb regenerates from a partial cross-section.
9.n: Series of partially-amputated Triton cristatus limbs
9.finding: A complete limb regenerates even from a partial (half) cross-section — evidence for a regulative 'determination field' that restores the whole pattern from incomplete material.
9.summary: Weiss showed that even a partial limb cross-section regenerates an entire limb, so the regenerate's pattern cannot be a simple copy of the stump's tissues. The result became a classic demonstration of regulative field behaviour, feeding directly into Bryant's double-half-limb experiments and the polar-coordinate model of limb pattern.

119.manipulation: Experimentally exchanged the reproductive mode of the alpine and fire salamanders (and the midwife toad) by altering their conditions, then bred the modified animals.
119.design: Experimentally altered animals and their offspring compared against controls across generations.
119.readout: Whether the enforced reproductive mode and associated characters reappear in the offspring.
119.n: Offspring series tabulated for both species
119.finding: Kammerer reported that the experimentally enforced reproductive adaptations were transmitted to the offspring (a Lamarckian / atavism claim, later disputed).
119.summary: This second communication extends Kammerer's program: he exchanged the reproductive mode between the two Salamandra species by manipulating their environment, then bred the altered animals and tabulated the offspring, reporting that the enforced adaptation was inherited. The corpus reports the claim as stated; it belongs to the contested neo-Lamarckian work at the centre of the modern epigenetics-revival debate.

81.manipulation: Applied adrenalin, pituitrin (infundin), pilocarpine, atropine and other drugs to minnows and watched the melanophores and xanthophores respond.
81.design: Series of chemical treatments versus the normal state; agonists and antagonists compared on the two chromatophore types.
81.readout: Expansion and contraction of melanophores and xanthophores and the resulting colour.
81.finding: Specific drugs expand or contract specific chromatophores, implicating adrenergic and cholinergic control of fish colour change.
81.summary: Abolin turned pharmacology on colour change, applying adrenalin, pituitrin, pilocarpine, atropine and others to the minnow and recording how the melanophores and xanthophores responded. By mapping which agents drove which pigment cells, he gave an early chemical dissection of the autonomic control of chromatophores that vertebrate colour-change physiology still pursues.

88.manipulation: Transplanted an eye onto a host salamander larva and followed the graft through metamorphosis.
88.design: Observation of the grafted eye's developmental timing relative to the host — a 'whole controls the part' test.
88.readout: Whether the transplanted eye metamorphoses in step with the host.
88.finding: The transplanted eye metamorphoses synchronously with its host: the organism as a whole imposes its developmental timing on the isolated part, pointing to a systemic (hormonal) control of metamorphosis.
88.summary: Uhlenhuth inverted Child's isolation logic: rather than freeing a part from the whole, he grafted an eye into a host and asked whether the whole could compel the part. The graft metamorphosed in exact step with its host, showing that a circulating systemic signal — not the organ's own clock — drives metamorphosis, an early pointer toward the thyroid control of amphibian metamorphosis.

11.manipulation: Tested what a regeneration blastema can form by placing tail-blastema material within the limb field in Triton.
11.design: Comparison of the structures formed by a blastema in its own field versus a foreign field.
11.readout: Whether tail-blastema forms limb structures when sited in the limb field.
11.finding: The blastema's fate follows the field it occupies — tail-blastema in the limb field can form limb structures — confirming that a regional 'determination field', not the blastema's origin, dictates the organ.
11.summary: Weiss probed the regeneration blastema's potency by moving tail-blastema into the limb region, finding that the surrounding 'limb field' redirects it toward limb formation. Together with his whole-from-half and skeleton-independence results, this fixed the regenerate's pattern as field-imposed — the field concept that runs forward into modern limb-regeneration biology.

164.manipulation: Produced 'artificial hermaphrodite glands' by implanting both sex glands, and compared their effects with naturally occurring hermaphrodite glands.
164.design: Artificial double-gland animals versus natural hermaphrodites; analysis of the 'antagonism of the sex hormones'.
164.readout: Secondary sex characters and their suppression or expression under opposing gland secretions.
164.finding: Male and female puberty-gland secretions antagonize one another, and artificial double-glands reproduce the effects of natural hermaphrodite glands — evidence for opposing sex hormones.
164.summary: Steinach implanted both sex glands to build 'artificial hermaphrodite glands' and set their effects beside those of naturally occurring intersex glands. The mutual suppression he observed — his 'antagonism of the sex hormones' — carried his puberty-gland theory toward the modern understanding of opposing androgenic and oestrogenic action.

98.manipulation: Transplanted the amphibian eye to a new site or host and followed its development and size.
98.design: Observation of the implanted eye's autonomy versus its dependence on the host (substrate, nutrition, spatial relations).
98.readout: The grafted eye's continued development and its size relative to the host.
98.finding: The transplanted eye largely develops autonomously, but the host whole modulates its size and development through nutritive and spatial relations.
98.summary: Uhlenhuth's eye-transplantation study weighed how far a grafted organ governs its own fate against how far the host whole — as a 'nursing mother' — shapes it. The eye kept much of its intrinsic development while the host adjusted its size: a Roux-style transplantation analysis of the balance between self-differentiation and dependence.

133.manipulation: Amputated at varied levels and sites in the adult newt and recorded which regions retain the capacity to regenerate a leg.
133.design: Systematic mapping of regenerative potency across the developed limb.
133.readout: Whether, and what kind of, leg regenerates from each amputation site.
133.finding: Maps the leg-forming potencies the developed newt limb retains — which tissues and levels can still rebuild a leg.
133.summary: Kurz charted the adult newt's leg-forming potencies, amputating at different levels to find where the capacity to regenerate a limb still resided. The work belongs to the BVA's sustained dissection of urodele limb regeneration — the same Triton system in which Weiss would later define the determination field.

170.manipulation: Reared cabbage-white pupae against different backgrounds and analysed the chemistry of the pigments behind their colour adaptation.
170.design: Correlation of background-induced colour with pigment chemistry, in the sixth instalment of the Pieris series.
170.readout: Pupal colour and the chemical nature of its pigments.
170.finding: Pinpoints the chemism — the pigment chemistry — underlying the pupa's environmentally cued colour adaptation.
170.summary: In the sixth part of her Pieris series Brecher pushed from describing pupal colour adaptation to its chemistry, relating the background-cued colour to the pigments that produce it. The work anchors the pupal-colour-plasticity and melanisation literature that still cites her by name.

174.manipulation: Surveyed and analysed cases where a break or wound triggers regeneration of mirror-symmetric triple appendages across the animal kingdom.
174.design: Comprehensive comparative survey plus analysis of the symmetry rule that governs triple formations.
174.readout: The geometry (mirror symmetry) and conditions of triple-limb formation.
174.finding: At a break, appendages regenerate as mirror-symmetric triples — a general morphogenetic rule (Bateson's rule) holding across the animal kingdom.
174.summary: Przibram's vast monograph generalizes 'triple-formation': when an appendage is broken, the regenerate often appears as a mirror-symmetric trio. Surveying the phenomenon across the animal kingdom, he frames it as a morphogenetic law — Bateson's rule of mirror symmetry — a pattern principle that still informs work on supernumerary limbs.

156.manipulation: Extracted substances from animals and tested them in preliminary experiments aimed at the causes of animal colouration.
156.design: Preliminary biochemical experiments on extracts as a first step toward the chemistry of colour.
156.readout: The colour-producing substances and their behaviour in extracts.
156.finding: Begins a biochemical analysis of the substances responsible for animal colouration.
156.summary: Przibram and Brecher opened a biochemical front on colour, extracting pigment-related substances from animals as a 'preliminary' first instalment toward the causes of colouration. It pairs the BVA's colour-adaptation program with the institute's physico-chemical bent, treating colour as a problem of chemistry as much as of stimulus and response.

86.manipulation: Induced colour change in salamanders by rearing them on differently coloured backgrounds, then bred the colour-altered animals.
86.design: Offspring of colour-modified animals compared with controls across generations.
86.readout: Whether the enforced colour change reappears in the offspring.
86.finding: Kammerer reported the enforced colour changes were inherited (a neo-Lamarckian claim, later disputed).
86.summary: Companion to his reproductive-adaptation work, Kammerer reared salamanders on coloured backgrounds to drive colour change and then bred them, reporting the change in their descendants — extending his contested environment-writes-heredity program to coloration.

59.manipulation: Reared the Egyptian praying mantis and observed its colour change and the regeneration of amputated parts.
59.design: Exploratory rearing-and-experiment study establishing the mantis as an experimental object.
59.readout: Background colour change and regeneration of amputated appendages.
59.finding: Established Sphodromantis as a tractable experimental insect, with notable colour change and regenerative capacity.
59.summary: Przibram's founding study domesticated the Egyptian praying mantis for the BVA, showing how to rear it and documenting its colour change and regeneration — the groundwork for the institute's long mantis program in growth, regeneration and colour.

92.manipulation: Exposed the cave-dwelling olm to altered conditions (notably light) and recorded effects on reproduction, colour, eyes and body form.
92.design: Altered-environment treatment versus the natural cave state.
92.readout: Reproduction, pigmentation, eye development and body reduction.
92.finding: Kammerer reported that environmental exposure elicits pigment and eye development in the otherwise pale, blind cave olm.
92.summary: Kammerer's olm experiments probed how fixed the cave salamander's pale, eyeless form is: exposing Proteus to light and altered conditions, he reported induced pigment and eye development, folding the result into his environment-shapes-the-organism program (claims later treated cautiously).

57.manipulation: Altered the breeding conditions (temperature, humidity) of the midwife toad and tree frog to change their reproductive behaviour.
57.design: Experimental alteration of reproductive activity versus normal behaviour.
57.readout: Mode and site of egg-laying and brood care.
57.finding: Environmental change forces the midwife toad and tree frog to alter how and where they breed — the induction step behind Kammerer's later inheritance claims.
57.summary: This earlier Kammerer paper established that manipulating temperature and humidity forces the midwife toad and tree frog to change their reproduction — the experimental induction that his 1909 work would then claim is inherited.

116.manipulation: Studied how pigment forms in amphibians, including through transplantation and observation of pigment-cell behaviour.
116.design: Experimental and observational study of pigment formation.
116.readout: Origin and development of pigment in the skin.
116.finding: Analyses the formation and origin of animal pigment, contributing to the developmental account of pigmentation.
116.summary: Winkler's studies on pigment formation tackled where and how animal pigment arises — part of the BVA's broad coloration program that also ran through Brecher's pupae and Megušar's crustaceans.

165.manipulation: Examined, microscopically, the histological structure of the testis in a sample of men described as homosexual.
165.finding: Steinach claimed a distinctive gonad histology in homosexual men, applying his puberty-gland theory to sexual orientation — a claim not supported by later science.
165.summary: Extending his hormonal theory of sex to human orientation, Steinach reported a distinctive testicular histology in homosexual men. Influential and troubling in its time, the claim is not supported by modern science; the paper is included here as a historical document of how the puberty-gland theory was over-extended.

48.manipulation: Amputated parts of amphibian larvae of different ages, developmental stages and body sizes and assessed their regenerative capacity.
48.design: Comparison of regeneration across age, stage and size classes.
48.readout: Extent and rate of regeneration as a function of larval age, stage and size.
48.finding: The regenerative capacity of amphibian larvae depends systematically on age, developmental stage and body size.
48.summary: Kammerer asked what governs how well an amphibian larva regenerates, amputating across age, stage and size classes and relating regenerative power to each — an early quantification of the conditions that set regenerative capacity.

69.manipulation: Cut or removed the nerve supply of developed amphibian limbs and tested whether they still regenerate after amputation.
69.design: Regeneration of denervated versus innervated limbs.
69.readout: Whether and how the limb regenerates without its nerves.
69.finding: Limb regeneration depends on the nervous system — denervated limbs fail to regenerate — a foundational result on the nerve-dependence of regeneration.
69.summary: Weiss's companion to his skeleton-independence paper showed the opposite necessity: cut the nerves and the limb will not regenerate. This nerve-dependence of regeneration became a cornerstone of regeneration biology, running forward to Singer's mid-century work on the neurotrophic control of regrowth.

49.manipulation: Studied 'heterochely' — the asymmetry of the two claws — in decapod crustaceans through claw-removal and regeneration.
49.design: Regeneration experiments on the paired claws (third communication of his regeneration studies).
49.readout: Which claw regenerates as the large versus the small form after removal.
49.finding: Removing a claw reorganizes the large/small asymmetry — regeneration governs the establishment of claw heterochely.
49.summary: In the third of his experimental regeneration studies, Przibram used crustacean claws to analyse 'heterochely' — why one claw is large and one small — showing that regeneration after claw loss reorganizes the asymmetry, an early model for how paired structures set their handedness.

125.manipulation: Amputated appendages of beetles and observed their regeneration across species.
125.readout: Capacity and pattern of regeneration of beetle appendages.
125.finding: Documents the regenerative capacities of beetles, mapping which appendages regenerate and how.
125.summary: Megušar surveyed regeneration in beetles, amputating appendages across species to chart their regenerative powers — part of the BVA's comparative regeneration program that spanned crustaceans, insects and amphibians.

159.manipulation: Reared cabbage-white pupae under different light and background conditions and recorded the colour they assumed.
159.design: Comparison of pupal colour across light/background treatments.
159.readout: Pupal colouration as a function of the surrounding light and background.
159.finding: Pupal colour adapts to the light and background — environmentally cued pupal colouration in Pieris.
159.summary: An earlier instalment of Brecher's Pieris series, establishing that the cabbage-white pupa's colour responds to the light and background it pupates against — the adaptive colour phenomenon her later 'chemism' parts would dissect chemically.

67.manipulation: Reared Pieris pupae against different backgrounds and light and recorded their colour.
67.design: Background and light treatment series across pupae.
67.finding: Confirms and extends the environmentally cued colour adaptation of the cabbage-white pupa.
67.summary: A 1917 instalment of Brecher's long Pieris pupal-colour study, documenting how the pupa matches its colour to its surroundings — groundwork for the pupal-colour-plasticity literature.

36.manipulation: Transplanted developed (adult) limbs onto amphibians and followed their incorporation.
36.design: Morphological study of how the grafted limb takes and develops.
36.readout: Survival, healing and structure of the transplanted limb.
36.finding: Developed amphibian limbs can be transplanted and incorporated — the morphological basis for Weiss's later functional and resonance studies.
36.summary: Weiss's 1923 paper established the surgery: transplanting fully formed amphibian limbs and showing that they take and integrate. It is the morphological precursor on which his 1924 'function of transplanted limbs' and the resonance principle would build.

41.manipulation: Reared praying mantises and observed their colour change and regeneration of amputated parts.
41.design: Exploratory rearing-and-experiment study of the mantids.
41.readout: Colour change and regeneration of amputated parts.
41.finding: Documents rearing, colour change and regeneration in praying mantises, extending the BVA's mantis program.
41.summary: Thomsen's study continued the BVA's work on praying mantises — how to rear them and their colour change and regeneration — paralleling Przibram's founding mantis paper and feeding the institute's sustained use of the mantis as a model.

110.manipulation: Experimentally manipulated the newts' gonads and observed the development of their secondary sexual characters (e.g., the breeding crest).
110.design: Comparison of secondary sex characters under gonadal manipulation versus normal.
110.readout: Development of secondary sexual characters in Triton.
110.finding: Links the newts' secondary sexual characters to gonadal control — an amphibian counterpart to Steinach's mammalian sex-gland work.
110.summary: Brescia's experiments on the secondary sexual characters of newts probed how the gonad controls the male breeding dress, an amphibian parallel to the BVA's broader endocrine-sex program led by Steinach.

127.manipulation: Bred Angora cats and tracked the inheritance of asymmetric eye colour (odd-eyed cats) across generations.
127.design: Breeding crosses analysed for the inheritance pattern of eye-colour asymmetry.
127.finding: Analyses how asymmetric (odd-eyed) eye coloration is inherited in Angora cats.
127.summary: Przibram turned breeding analysis on a charismatic trait — the odd-eyed Angora cat — tracking how asymmetric eye colour passes through generations, a Mendelian-era heredity study within the BVA's inheritance program.

42.manipulation: Subjected the stone loach to colour-change experiments, varying background and light and watching its chromatophores.
42.design: Background and light treatment series on the fish.
42.finding: The stone loach changes colour with its background and light via chromatophore adjustment.
42.summary: Secerov's colour-change experiments on the stone loach documented how the fish matches its background through chromatophore expansion and contraction — part of the BVA's vertebrate colour-change line that Abolín would later probe pharmacologically.

149.manipulation: Amputated and observed regeneration on previously transplanted (grafted) amphibian limbs.
149.design: Regeneration tested on transplanted versus normal limbs.
149.readout: Whether and how a transplanted limb regenerates after amputation.
149.finding: Transplanted limbs retain the capacity to regenerate, tying transplantation and regeneration in the same system.
149.summary: Weiss combined his two tools — transplantation and regeneration — amputating grafted limbs to ask whether a transplanted limb can still regenerate. The result linked the regenerative field to the transplanted organ, part of his sustained analysis of limb determination.

157.manipulation: Investigated the causes of animal colouration, including the chemical substances behind colour.
157.design: Experimental and analytical study of the causes of colour.
157.readout: The substances and mechanisms that produce animal colour.
157.finding: Frames animal colouration as a problem of underlying causes — pigment chemistry and physiology — companion to the Przibram-Brecher extract experiments.
157.summary: Przibram's 'causes of animal colouration' sets the agenda for treating colour as a chemical-physiological problem, the companion framing to the Przibram-Brecher extract experiments translated on this site, and a rediscovery target whose organism (the mantis) is still studied.

169.manipulation: Exposed animals (rats) to elevated temperature ('climate') and assessed the effect on the puberty gland and sexual maturity ('virility').
169.design: Heat treatment versus control; effect on gonadal and sexual development.
169.readout: Sexual maturity and puberty-gland condition under thermal stress.
169.finding: Steinach and Kammerer linked elevated temperature ('climate') to changes in the puberty gland and sexual maturation — climate acting on the endocrine sex system.
169.summary: 'Klima und Mannbarkeit' joined the BVA's two sex-and-environment programs: Steinach's puberty gland and Kammerer's environmental induction. Exposing rats to heat, they argued that climate acts on the puberty gland to shape sexual maturity — work later read critically (Logan's 'overheated rats') for its racial and Lamarckian framing.

172.manipulation: Reared cabbage-white pupae under controlled light, testing the specific action of different spectral regions against other factors (fifth part of the series).
172.design: Control experiments isolating the spectral region responsible for pupal colour from confounding factors.
172.readout: Pupal colour as a function of the spectral region of light.
172.finding: Pins the pupal colour response to specific spectral regions of light, controlling for other factors.
172.summary: The fifth instalment of Brecher's Pieris series ran control experiments to isolate which spectral regions of light drive the pupa's colour, separating the light effect from other rearing factors — tightening the causal account of environmentally cued pupal colour.

153.manipulation: Measured and compared the rate of regeneration against normal growth, treating regeneration as accelerated growth.
153.design: Quantitative comparison of regenerative and normal growth rates.
153.readout: Rate and time-course of regeneration relative to growth.
153.finding: Regeneration is, quantitatively, a temporary acceleration of normal growth — unifying the two processes.
153.summary: Przibram reframed regeneration as 'growth acceleration', arguing on quantitative grounds that the fast tissue increase after injury is the organism's normal growth speeded up. The thesis tied the BVA's regeneration and biometric-growth programs into one rate-based account.

167.manipulation: Cultured pure-bred lines of the ciliate Paramecium aurelia and measured body-size variation in relation to endomixis (periodic nuclear reorganization).
167.design: Biometry of size across the endomixis cycle within genetically pure lines.
167.readout: Body-size distribution and its variation through endomixis.
167.finding: Body size varies systematically with the endomixis cycle in pure lines — linking a nuclear-reorganization rhythm to biometric size variation.
167.summary: Erdmann brought pure-line genetics and biometry to the ciliate Paramecium, tracking how body size varies with 'endomixis', the periodic nuclear reorganization she invoked to explain rejuvenation in clonal lines. The work sits in the Jennings-era debate over variation and inheritance in protozoan pure lines.

140.manipulation: Took serial measurements of the growth of the praying mantis Sphodromantis bioculata.
140.design: Longitudinal biometry of growth across instars.
140.readout: Body dimensions over time and across moults.
140.finding: Quantifies the mantis growth curve, instar by instar.
140.summary: Sztern's growth measurements on the mantis fed Przibram's quantitative-growth program directly, charting how Sphodromantis increases in size through its moults — the kind of biometric growth law the BVA sought across its model organisms.

10.manipulation: Determined where the skin covering a limb regenerate comes from, using grafting and marking on the regenerating amphibian limb.
10.design: Traced whether regenerate skin derives from the surrounding old skin or from deeper tissue.
10.readout: Tissue source of the epidermis / skin on the regenerate.
10.finding: Establishes the origin of the skin in the limb regenerate, clarifying which tissue supplies the new epidermis.
10.summary: Weiss asked a precise lineage question — where does the skin on a regenerated limb come from — using grafting and marking to trace its source. It refined the cellular bookkeeping of amphibian limb regeneration within his broader field-and-determination program.

73.manipulation: Cut Hydra (Pelmatohydra oligactis) into head and foot regions and tested what each region can regenerate.
73.design: Regeneration of isolated head and foot pieces — a unipotency / polarity test.
73.finding: Head and foot regions behave as unipotent systems, each regenerating along its own polarity — axial determination in Hydra.
73.summary: Burt treated the freshwater Hydra's head and foot as 'unipotent systems', cutting and observing what each end regenerates to probe the polarity of the body axis. The study belongs to the classic line on Hydra regeneration and axial gradients that runs to modern work on head and foot organizers.

141.manipulation: Reared cabbage-white pupae under different light and background and recorded their colour (the main paper of the series).
141.design: Background and light treatment series on Pieris pupae.
141.readout: Pupal colour versus light and background.
141.finding: Establishes the environmentally cued colour adaptation of the cabbage-white pupa.
141.summary: A central instalment of Brecher's long Pieris study, documenting how the cabbage-white pupa tunes its colour to the surrounding light and background — the core phenomenon her control and 'chemism' papers would then dissect by spectrum and by pigment chemistry.

158.manipulation: Tested how excluding air (oxygen) preserves the pigment enzyme tyrosinase in animal extracts.
158.design: Comparison of tyrosinase activity with versus without air exclusion.
158.readout: Persistence of tyrosinase, and so of pigment-forming capacity.
158.finding: Excluding air conserves tyrosinase — the melanin-forming enzyme is oxygen-sensitive and can be preserved anaerobically.
158.summary: Przibram and Dembowski took the BVA's colour program into enzymology, showing that the pigment-forming enzyme tyrosinase is preserved when air is excluded. With Weindl's preformed-tyrosinase paper it grounded animal coloration in a specific, manipulable enzyme chemistry.

40.manipulation: Ran colour-change experiments on the pike, varying background and light and watching its chromatophores.
40.design: Background and light treatment series on the fish.
40.finding: The pike changes colour with its background and light via chromatophore adjustment.
40.summary: Mayerhofer's pike experiments extended the BVA's vertebrate colour-change line to a large predatory fish, documenting how it matches its background — a companion to Secerov's loach and Abolin's minnow pharmacology.

60.manipulation: Compared the growth, restitution and form of crystals with those of organisms as a physical model for developmental mechanics.
60.design: Conceptual and analogical analysis treating crystals as models of the organism.
60.readout: Parallels between crystal and organismic growth, regeneration and symmetry.
60.finding: Crystals furnish physical analogies for organic growth, regeneration and symmetry — a reductionist frame for Entwicklungsmechanik.
60.summary: Przibram's 'crystal analogies' argued that the physics of crystal growth and restitution can model organic development, regeneration and symmetry. It is the theoretical signature of his physico-chemical program — reading life's form-making as continuous with the inorganic world.

64.manipulation: Exposed animals to altered temperature and examined whether the environment reaches and modifies the germ-plasm.
64.design: Thermal treatment versus control, with attention to heritable (germ-plasm) effects.
64.readout: Effects of the environment on the germ-plasm and on offspring.
64.finding: Argues that the environment — notably temperature — penetrates to and modifies the germ-plasm, a channel for environmental effects on heredity.
64.summary: 'Die Umwelt des Keimplasmas' is Przibram's theoretical-experimental case that the environment is not sealed off from the germ-plasm: temperature and surroundings can reach it. The thesis sits at the BVA's Lamarckian edge, the conceptual partner to Kammerer's inheritance experiments and to Congdon's parallel 'surroundings of the germ plasm'.

96.manipulation: Examined how the surroundings, including temperature, relate to and affect the germ plasm.
96.design: Analysis of environmental influence on the germ plasm.
96.readout: Whether and how the surroundings modify the germ plasm.
96.finding: Treats the germ plasm as influenced by its surroundings — environment as a factor in heredity.
96.summary: Congdon's 'surroundings of the germ plasm' shares its title and theme with Przibram's 'Umwelt des Keimplasmas': both ask whether the hereditary substance is shielded from or open to the environment. The BVA included it as a kindred statement in its environment-and-heredity debate.

138.manipulation: Tested whether gill resorption at metamorphosis in the fire salamander depends on the whole organism, by isolating and grafting tissue.
138.design: Gill regression in isolated or grafted tissue versus in the intact animal.
138.readout: Whether the gills regress on schedule away from the whole organism.
138.finding: The metamorphic resorption of the gills depends on the whole organism — a systemic (hormonal) signal, not the gill's own programme, drives it.
138.summary: Kornfeld showed that the fire salamander's gills do not regress on their own clock: their metamorphic resorption depends on the whole organism. Like Uhlenhuth's synchronously metamorphosing eye graft, the result pointed to a circulating (thyroid) control of amphibian metamorphosis.

21.manipulation: Reared the pupae of the tortoiseshell, peacock and painted-lady butterflies (Vanessids) under different light and recorded their colour.
21.design: Light and background treatment series across several Vanessid species.
21.readout: Pupal colour versus light and background.
21.n: Vanessa io, V. urticae, Pyrameis cardui, P. atalanta pupae
21.finding: Vanessid pupae adapt their colour to light and background, extending the Pieris result to other butterflies.
21.summary: Brecher carried her pupal-colour analysis from the cabbage white to the Vanessid butterflies, showing the same environmentally cued colour adaptation across peacock, tortoiseshell and painted-lady pupae — evidence the phenomenon is general among butterflies.

107.manipulation: Amputated appendages of bristletails (Lepismatidae) and observed their regeneration, drawing conclusions of general significance.
107.design: Regeneration experiments in apterygote insects that continue moulting as adults.
107.readout: Capacity and pattern of appendage regeneration.
107.finding: Bristletails regenerate appendages well — their lifelong moulting makes them a generally informative model for regeneration.
107.summary: Przibram used the bristletails (silverfish and kin) — wingless insects that keep moulting as adults — to draw regeneration conclusions 'of more general significance'. A rediscovery target, it chose an unusual model precisely because continued moulting keeps regenerative capacity open.

108.manipulation: Investigated pigment formation as the action of preformed tyrosinase enzymes on their substrate.
108.design: Biochemical account of pigment arising from pre-existing enzymes.
108.finding: Pigment arises through preformed tyrosinase enzymes — an enzymatic theory of animal pigmentation.
108.summary: Weindl argued that animal pigment forms when preformed tyrosinase enzymes act on their substrate, an early enzymatic theory of pigmentation. It anchors the BVA's pigment-chemistry strand — later joined by Przibram and Dembowski's work on conserving the same enzyme.

114.manipulation: Amputated the raptorial, walking and jumping legs of reared orthopterans and followed their regeneration.
114.design: Comparative regeneration of functionally distinct leg types during rearing.
114.finding: Documents how the different specialized legs of orthopterans regenerate when the animals are reared from young.
114.summary: Megušar compared regeneration across the orthopteran's specialized legs — the mantis's grasping foreleg, the walking and the jumping legs — exploiting the BVA's rearing methods. With his beetle paper it mapped insect regeneration across body parts and taxa.

8.manipulation: Tested the developmental potency of the regeneration blastema in the lizard (Lacerta).
8.design: Potency-probing of the blastema (transposition / challenge), as in his Triton work, now in a reptile.
8.finding: Probes how far the reptilian regeneration blastema is determined versus plastic — extending the determination-field analysis beyond amphibians.
8.summary: Weiss carried his blastema-potency analysis into the lizard, testing how determined the reptilian regenerate is. A rediscovery target, it extends the field-and-determination framework — built on Triton — to a reptile that regenerates its tail.

25.manipulation: Removed and re-implanted ('replanted') eyes and followed their survival, structure and function.
25.design: Replantation of the eye, observing reconnection and recovery.
25.readout: Survival, healing and functional recovery of the replanted eye.
25.finding: Eyes can be replanted and re-establish structure and some function — early evidence for the eye's capacity to reconnect.
25.summary: Koppanyi's eye-replantation experiments removed and re-set eyes to ask whether they survive and function again, part of the BVA's deep vein of eye-transplantation work alongside Uhlenhuth's grafts. The line fed Koppanyi's later studies of vision and optic-nerve reconnection.

111.manipulation: Studied the colour-change reactions of the prawn Palaemon and the conditions (light, physiological state) that drive its chromatophores.
111.design: Observation of chromatophore responses under varied conditions.
111.readout: Chromatophore expansion and contraction and the resulting colour change.
111.finding: Characterizes the prawn's colour-change reactions — an early analysis of crustacean chromatophore control.
111.summary: Fröhlich's study of colour-change reactions in the prawn Palaemon opened the crustacean side of the BVA's colour-change physiology, charting how the animal's chromatophores respond. The crustacean colour-change system he probed would later be explained by chromatophorotropic hormones.

115.manipulation: Surveyed and analysed 'homeosis' in arthropods — cases where one body part is replaced by the form of another (e.g., an antenna regenerating as a leg).
115.design: Comparative analysis of homeotic replacements across arthropods, often after regeneration.
115.readout: Which part is replaced by which, and under what conditions.
115.finding: Homeosis — the substitution of one appendage type for another — is a regular, analysable phenomenon in arthropods, frequently revealed through regeneration.
115.summary: Przibram's study of arthropod homeosis catalogued the striking cases where a regenerate appears as the wrong organ — an antenna as a leg, an eye as an antenna. Taking up Bateson's term, it framed homeosis as a window on the rules of pattern, a theme that runs straight to the modern homeotic-gene literature.

12.manipulation: Measured the body temperature of young brown rats and how it is shifted by the temperature of the surrounding world.
12.design: Body temperature recorded across a range of ambient temperatures.
12.readout: Body temperature as a function of external temperature.
12.finding: Young rats are not yet fully homeothermic — their body temperature tracks the ambient temperature.
12.summary: De Hahn measured how far the body temperature of young rats is at the mercy of their surroundings, showing the neonate's incomplete thermoregulation. The work fits the BVA's interest in how external conditions reach into the organism's physiology.

37.manipulation: Transplanted developed (formed) limbs in amphibians and followed their take and development.
37.design: Observation of how grafted adult limbs heal in and develop.
37.readout: Survival, integration and structure of the transplanted limb.
37.finding: Developed amphibian limbs can be transplanted and incorporated — the surgical foundation for Weiss's functional and field studies.
37.summary: Weiss's full account of transplanting developed amphibian limbs established the technique on which his later work — the function of transplanted limbs, the resonance principle, the determination field — would all rest. It is the methodological keystone of his BVA programme.

18.manipulation: Reversed the orientation of the newt leg and tested whether its polarity (proximo-distal axis) could be overturned.
18.design: Regeneration / growth from reversed limb segments versus normal.
18.readout: The axis along which the reversed limb regenerates or grows.
18.finding: Probes whether limb polarity is fixed or reversible — a direct test of how firmly the proximo-distal axis is determined.
18.summary: Kurz tried to turn the newt's leg against its own axis, reversing segments to see whether the limb's polarity would follow the graft or hold to its origin. The experiment belongs to the BVA's sustained inquiry — culminating in Weiss — into how the limb's axes are fixed.

131.manipulation: Measured how regeneration affects the growth rate of the water louse Asellus aquaticus.
131.design: Growth rate of regenerating versus intact animals.
131.readout: Growth velocity with and without an ongoing regeneration.
131.finding: Regeneration alters (accelerates) the growth rate of the isopod — an empirical case for the regeneration-as-growth link.
131.summary: Zuelzer measured the growth of the water louse to see what an ongoing regeneration does to it, finding that regeneration and growth are coupled. The result is one of the concrete observations behind Przibram's later thesis that regeneration is growth accelerated.

147.manipulation: Investigated the role of DOPA in the cocoons of certain moths and sawflies, locating the chemical sites of melanin formation.
147.design: Biochemical analysis of where and how melanin forms via DOPA.
147.readout: Presence of DOPA and the sites of melanin synthesis in the cocoon.
147.finding: DOPA participates in melanin formation in insect cocoons, and the chemical sites of melanisation can be localized.
147.summary: Przibram traced the chemistry of insect cocoon pigment to DOPA, pinpointing where melanin is made — an advance on the BVA's tyrosinase work toward the now-standard tyrosinase/DOPA melanin pathway. It shows the institute's colour program reaching real biochemical specificity.

160.manipulation: Enforced changes of body form by altered conditions, then bred the modified animals to test transmission.
160.design: Offspring of form-modified animals versus controls across generations.
160.readout: Whether the enforced form change reappears in the offspring.
160.finding: Kammerer reported that experimentally enforced changes of form were inherited (a neo-Lamarckian claim, later disputed).
160.summary: The form-change counterpart to Kammerer's colour and reproduction papers: he claimed that body-shape changes forced by the environment pass to the offspring. It belongs to the contested neo-Lamarckian work now revisited in the epigenetics-revival debate.

161.manipulation: Removed the amphibian lens and followed its regeneration from the iris, with attention to the depigmentation of the iris margin (first study of the series).
161.design: Observation of Wolffian lens regeneration and the iris changes that accompany it.
161.readout: Regrowth of a new lens from the dorsal iris and the iris's depigmentation.
161.finding: The amphibian regenerates a new lens from the depigmenting dorsal iris — the classic case of Wolffian lens regeneration, here with critical remarks on stimulus physiology.
161.summary: Uhlenhuth opened his lens-regeneration series on the textbook case of Wolffian regeneration: a removed lens is rebuilt from the iris, whose cells lose their pigment as they convert. His critical 'remarks on the value of stimulus physiology' show the BVA arguing over how such regeneration should be explained.

168.manipulation: Continued the study of lens regeneration from the iris in amphibians.
168.design: Observation of the course and conditions of lens regeneration.
168.readout: Formation of a new lens from iris tissue.
168.finding: Extends the analysis of how the iris regenerates a lens — the conditions and tissue conversion involved in Wolffian regeneration.
168.summary: The continuation of Uhlenhuth's lens-regeneration studies pressed further into how the iris remakes a lens, a model of cell-type conversion (transdifferentiation) that remains a touchstone in regeneration biology.

50.manipulation: Removed the jaw of the lizard Lacerta agilis and followed its regeneration.
50.design: Observation of jaw regeneration after excision.
50.readout: Extent and structure of the regenerated jaw.
50.finding: The lizard can regenerate jaw structures after their removal — extending reptilian regeneration beyond the familiar tail.
50.summary: Werber showed the lizard regenerating its jaw, widening the known reach of reptilian regeneration. A rediscovery target, it pairs with the BVA's other Lacerta work and with Werber's beetle-regeneration studies.

62.manipulation: Analysed the 'disordered' (random) movement of lower animals as a statistical, physics-like process (second communication).
62.design: Quantitative analysis of movement paths against a random-motion model.
62.readout: The statistics of the animals' trajectories.
62.finding: The movement of lower organisms can be described as a disordered, quasi-random process — a physics of behaviour.
62.summary: Karl Przibram, the physicist of the Przibram brothers, treated the wandering of small animals as a random-walk problem, bringing the mathematics of disordered motion to biology. It is a striking instance of the BVA's physico-chemical, quantitative ethos applied to behaviour.

82.manipulation: Analysed the axis-relations and developmental potencies of urodele limbs using models built to interpret Harrison's transplantation experiments.
82.design: Model-based analysis of how the limb's axes and potencies are arranged.
82.readout: The arrangement of limb axes and the potencies along them.
82.finding: Frames how the urodele limb's three axes and developmental potencies are organized, interpreting Harrison's grafting results.
82.summary: Przibram engaged Ross Harrison's celebrated limb-transplantation work with explicit models of the limb's axes and potencies, asking how the three axes of the limb are laid down. The paper places the BVA in direct dialogue with the American school of experimental embryology.

83.manipulation: Argued, in the light of experimental morphology, that external living conditions act as a cause of organic variation.
83.design: Synthetic / programmatic statement drawing the experimental evidence together.
83.readout: The link between external conditions and organic variation.
83.finding: External living conditions are presented as a driver of organic variation — the theoretical core of Kammerer's environmental programme.
83.summary: This is Kammerer's manifesto: external conditions, he argued, are not a backdrop but a cause of the organism's variation, and experimental morphology is the way to prove it. It states the rationale behind his inheritance experiments and the BVA's environment-shapes-the-organism stance.

84.manipulation: Mapped how form-building (regenerative) capacity is distributed along the dorso-ventral axis of the animal body.
84.design: Comparison of form-building capacity at dorsal versus ventral sites.
84.readout: Regenerative / form-building capacity as a function of dorso-ventral position.
84.finding: Form-building capacity is unevenly distributed along the dorso-ventral axis — morphogenetic potency is regionally graded.
84.summary: Przibram charted where in the body the power to rebuild form is concentrated, finding a dorso-ventral gradient of morphogenetic capacity. It is a piece of the BVA's broader effort to map the body's restitution potencies — the same field-thinking Weiss would later formalize.

94.manipulation: Argued that the environment reaches and can modify the germ-plasm (the original 1912 statement).
94.design: Theoretical-experimental analysis of environmental influence on the germ-plasm.
94.readout: Whether the surroundings can affect the hereditary substance.
94.finding: Holds that the germ-plasm is open to its environment — a channel for environmental effects on heredity.
94.summary: This 1912 'Umwelt des Keimplasmas' is Przibram's original case that the hereditary substance is not sealed off from the world — later reissued (1917) and echoed in Congdon's English 'surroundings of the germ plasm'. It is the conceptual scaffold for the BVA's Lamarckian-leaning inheritance work.

97.manipulation: Ran further colour-change experiments on the stone loach and transplanted skin between regions/animals to test how colour control is organized.
97.design: Colour-change series combined with skin grafts (local versus systemic control).
97.readout: Colour change of grafted versus host skin.
97.finding: Skin grafts plus colour-change tests probe whether the fish's colour response is local to the skin or imposed by the whole animal.
97.summary: Secerov followed his first loach paper by adding skin transplantation to the colour-change experiment, asking whether a piece of skin keeps its own colour rule or obeys its new host. It sharpened the question of local versus systemic control that runs through the BVA's colour physiology.

128.manipulation: Studied 'claw reversal' in decapod crustaceans — how the large and small claws swap roles after loss (fourth communication of his regeneration studies).
128.design: Regeneration of the paired claws after removal, tracking which becomes large.
128.readout: Which claw regenerates as the large versus the small form.
128.finding: After losing the large claw, the remaining small claw can take over as the large one and the regenerate becomes the small — the asymmetry reverses.
128.summary: The fourth of Przibram's regeneration studies dissected 'claw reversal': remove a lobster's big claw and the small one enlarges while the regenerate stays small. With his heterochely paper it made the crustacean claw a model for how paired structures negotiate their asymmetry.

171.manipulation: Related the background-cued colour of cabbage-white pupae to the chemistry of their pigments (sixth part: 'chemism of colour adaptation').
171.design: Correlation of environmentally induced pupal colour with pigment chemistry.
171.readout: Pupal colour and the chemical nature of its pigments.
171.finding: Locates the 'chemism' — the pigment chemistry — behind the pupa's environmentally cued colour adaptation.
171.summary: The sixth part of Brecher's Pieris series turned from describing pupal colour adaptation to its chemistry, tying the background-cued colour to the pigments that make it. It anchors the pupal-colour-plasticity and melanisation literature that still cites the series by name.

17.manipulation: Reared the pupae of Vanessid butterflies under different light and recorded their colour (companion communication).
17.design: Light and background treatment series across Vanessid species.
17.readout: Pupal colour versus light and background.
17.n: Vanessa io, V. urticae, Pyrameis cardui, P. atalanta pupae
17.finding: Vanessid pupae match their colour to light and background, as the cabbage white does.
17.summary: A companion to her other Vanessid communication, this Brecher paper documents environmentally cued pupal colour across the peacock, tortoiseshell and painted-lady, showing the adaptation is general among butterflies and not peculiar to Pieris.

22.manipulation: Measured the change in the amount of melanin during the colour change of several fishes (Esox, Carassius, Phoxinus, Gobius, Nemachilus).
22.design: Comparison of melanin quantity between pale and dark states across species.
22.readout: Amount of melanin as the fish changes colour.
22.finding: The quantity of melanin itself changes during colour change — not only its distribution within chromatophores.
22.summary: Kudo asked whether fish colour change is only the spreading and clumping of pigment or also a change in how much melanin there is, and measured the pigment across species to find out. A rediscovery target, it pushed the BVA's colour-change work toward quantitative pigment chemistry.

71.manipulation: Induced and followed the lateral (side) regeneration of the urodele limb.
71.design: Regeneration from lateral surfaces of the limb versus from a clean amputation.
71.readout: What regenerates from a lateral wound on the limb.
71.finding: The urodele limb can regenerate from its lateral surfaces, revealing the spatial reach of its regenerative field.
71.summary: Weiss probed how far around the limb the capacity to regenerate extends, eliciting growth from lateral wounds rather than the cut tip. The result fed his analysis of the limb's determination field — where, and in what orientation, the limb can rebuild itself.

91.manipulation: Removed the reproductive organs of the earthworm Criodrilus lacuum and followed their regeneration (first communication).
91.design: Observation of how the genital apparatus is rebuilt after removal.
91.finding: The earthworm regenerates its reproductive organs — an unusually demanding restitution of a whole organ system.
91.summary: Janda showed the annelid Criodrilus rebuilding its sex organs after their removal, an exacting case of organ-system regeneration. It extends the BVA's regeneration programme from limbs and appendages to the reproductive apparatus of a worm.

100.manipulation: Crossed the perch (Perca fluviatilis) with the ruffe (Acerina cernua) and examined the hybrids.
100.design: Interspecies cross with description of the resulting hybrids.
100.readout: Viability and characters of the perch x ruffe hybrids.
100.finding: Perch and ruffe can be hybridized, and the hybrids show an intermediate or distinctive character set.
100.summary: Kammerer crossed two percid fishes to make perch x ruffe hybrids, testing the limits of interspecies crossing and the inheritance of characters across a species boundary. It is the hybridization wing of the BVA's inheritance programme.

117.manipulation: Exposed protozoa to seawater and recorded the effect on the pulsation of the contractile (pulsating) vacuole.
117.design: Contractile-vacuole activity in seawater versus fresh water.
117.readout: Rate or persistence of vacuole pulsation under raised salinity.
117.finding: Seawater slows or stops the contractile vacuole — the organelle's pulsation tracks the osmotic load, evidence of its osmoregulatory role.
117.summary: Zuelzer used seawater to challenge the protozoan contractile vacuole, showing its pulsation depends on the osmotic environment. The experiment is an early, clean demonstration of the contractile vacuole as an osmoregulatory organ.

51.manipulation: Removed the antenna and the eye of the mealworm beetle (Tenebrio molitor) and followed their regeneration.
51.design: Observation of antenna and eye regeneration after excision.
51.readout: Extent and structure of the regenerated antenna and eye.
51.finding: The mealworm beetle regenerates excised sense organs — antenna and eye — within its moulting cycle.
51.summary: Werber removed the beetle's antenna and eye to test how far insect sense organs regenerate, finding real restitution in Tenebrio. With his other beetle papers it built the BVA's case that holometabolous insects, too, retain regenerative power.

65.manipulation: Documented the transitory claw forms that the fiddler crab Gelasimus pugnax passes through (during regeneration / growth of the great claw).
65.design: Tracking the succession of intermediate claw forms.
65.readout: The sequence of transitory shapes the regenerating/growing claw assumes.
65.finding: The fiddler crab's great claw passes through characteristic transitory forms — a developmental sequence visible in regeneration.
65.summary: Przibram followed the fiddler crab's oversized claw through the intermediate shapes it takes on, reading the succession as a record of how the structure is built. It is a further chapter in his long crustacean-claw analysis of regeneration and asymmetry.

129.manipulation: Removed the wings of the mealworm beetle (Tenebrio molitor) and followed their regeneration.
129.design: Observation of wing regeneration after excision.
129.readout: Extent and structure of the regenerated wings.
129.finding: The beetle can regenerate excised wings — a notable case of appendage regeneration in a holometabolous insect.
129.summary: Werber tested whether the mealworm beetle could rebuild removed wings, and reported regeneration. A rediscovery target, it sits with his antenna/eye and Lacerta papers in the BVA's comparative survey of who can regenerate what.

132.manipulation: Followed the regeneration of the reproductive organs in the earthworm Criodrilus lacuum (companion communication).
132.design: Observation of how the genital apparatus is rebuilt after removal.
132.finding: Confirms and details the earthworm's capacity to regenerate its reproductive organs.
132.summary: A companion to his first Criodrilus paper, Janda further documented the annelid's regeneration of its sex organs — extending the BVA's regeneration programme to the reproductive system of a worm.

150.manipulation: Autotransplanted the urodele testis and checked whether spermatogenesis is maintained in the graft.
150.design: Spermatogenesis in the autografted testis versus normal.
150.readout: Whether sperm production continues in the transplanted testis.
150.finding: Spermatogenesis persists in the autoplastically transplanted testis — the organ retains its germinal function when moved.
150.summary: Koppanyi showed that a urodele testis grafted back into the same animal keeps making sperm, demonstrating the organ's autonomy under transplantation. It joins the BVA's gonad-transplantation line (Steinach, Koppanyi's eye work) bearing on how far an organ governs its own function.

154.manipulation: Examined cases in which raptorial (grasping) legs appear as regenerates in the mantis — a heteromorphic regeneration.
154.design: Analysis of when a regenerate takes the form of a raptorial leg.
154.readout: The identity of the regenerated appendage (raptorial leg vs other).
154.finding: Raptorial legs can be produced as regenerates, a homeotic/heteromorphic outcome of regeneration in the mantis.
154.summary: Przibram's 'raptorial legs as regenerates' documents the mantis regenerating a grasping foreleg where another part stood — a homeotic substitution. It ties his mantis programme to his work on homeosis and the rules that govern what a regenerate becomes.
